document:
Kilo represents the editable file as an in-memory array of `erow` structures. Each row carries the original characters of the line, a parallel rendered version used for display, syntax-highlight metadata, and a small flag tracking whether the previous line ended inside a multiline comment. The rendered view exists because what shows on screen is not always the same as what is on disk: most importantly, tab characters in the original buffer are expanded into spaces up to the next eight-column tab stop, so cursor positioning and highlighting line up with the visible output. Whenever a row's underlying characters change, `editorUpdateRow` rebuilds the rendered string and recomputes the highlight bytes for that row.

Inserting a row through `editorInsertRow` reallocates the row array, shifts later rows down with their indices fixed, initializes the new row's buffers, recomputes the rendered view, and marks the file dirty. Deleting a row through `editorDelRow` mirrors that work in reverse: `editorFreeRow` releases the row's heap allocations for the original text, the rendered buffer, and the highlight bytes, the rows above shift down, and the dirty flag is set.

Character-level editing splits cleanly into a row-scoped helper and an editor-scoped wrapper. `editorRowInsertChar` inserts a single character into one row, growing or shrinking that row's buffer and refreshing its rendered form. If the requested column is past the current end of the line, it first pads the row with spaces up to that column. `editorInsertChar` translates the visible cursor and scroll offsets into a file row and column, creates an empty row at the end of the file when typing past the last line, calls into the row helper, and advances the cursor.

Inserting a newline through `editorInsertNewline` either splits the current row at the cursor or inserts an empty row, depending on where the cursor sits. A cursor in the middle of a line breaks the row in two: the right half becomes a new row beneath the original, the original is truncated and its render data refreshed, and the cursor moves to the start of the new line. A cursor at column zero pushes a fresh empty row in front of the current one, and a cursor at the very end of the file gets a brand-new empty row to type into.

Backspace runs the same logic in reverse through `editorDelChar`. At the very start of the file it does nothing. When the cursor is past the first column it deletes the character to its left and steps back. When the cursor is at column zero it joins the current row to the previous one by appending the text and then deleting the original.

File loading goes through `editorOpen`, which clears the dirty flag, records the filename, and tries to open the path. A missing file is not an error: kilo returns success, leaves the buffer empty, and lets the user save under that name later. For an existing file, kilo reads line by line, strips trailing newlines, and inserts each line as a new `erow`.

Saving turns the in-memory rows back into a single block of text. `editorRowsToString` concatenates all rows with a newline between each. `editorSave` then opens or creates the target file, truncates it to the exact new length, writes the buffer in one call, and closes the file. The dirty flag clears on success and the editor reports how many bytes were written; on an I/O failure it leaves the dirty flag set and surfaces the error in the status line.
